words: This conversation with Clarice Moody Sampson took place in her home in Moscow,
Idaho on November 16, 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.
CLARICE M. SAMPSON: — buying one sack of flour, he a barrel; four sacks.
So, he had a great, big container made that was in the butry. Great
big, tight square box, the lid just fit in, you could hardly get it out.
And that was filled up with flour when the- before the new flour came
in; or did he want the new flour in the fall? I can't remember what
the rule was. And sugar by the hundred pounds, (clock chiming drowns
out voice)

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that he was that way because he had been close to not

speaker: SS
words: having enough at times?

speaker: CS
words: I think probably. He ran away from home when he was thirteen and went

speaker: CS
words: on his own. And the family lived near St. John, New Brunswick, and he

speaker: CS
words: went down to Boston. And that kid was on his own. So I don't know

speaker: CS
words: how he fared, he never would talk about it to us.

speaker: SS
words: So maybe they were pretty difficult times.

speaker: CS
words: And also, I know he'd never let us go barefoot, even in the summer on

speaker: CS
words: the grass, 'cause we'd stub our toes. So evidently he vent without

speaker: CS
words: shoes time. But Mama'd let us take off our shoes sometimes in

speaker: CS
words: the afternoon, but we sure had to get our feet washed and our shoes on

speaker: CS
words: fore Dad came home.

speaker: SS
words: So he didn't even know about it then.

speaker: CS
words: No, evidently not.

speaker: SS
words: So, I'll bet you really wanted to go without shoes.

speaker: CS
words: Oh, yes,'the hot days, you know. But we had board sidewalks and you

speaker: CS
words: could stub your toes so easily, you know.

speaker: SS
words: You know, you said to Laura that sometimes in the family, you said that

speaker: SS
words: the boys were closer to their mama and the girls were closer to their

speaker: SS
words: father.

speaker: CS
words: Well, it seemed that way to the girls in the family- and I don't know

speaker: CS
words: but it just seemed to me that Mama always favored the boys

speaker: CS
words: Of course, they were all older than I, and I'm really not the

speaker: CS
words: one to judge.

speaker: SS
words: It wouldn't be unusual, that that was true, because it seems to me that

speaker: SS
words: the girls often did seem closer to their father. Did you feel close to

speaker: SS
words: him?

speaker: CS
words: Uh-huh. Very. I think that I was quite the apple of his eye when I

speaker: CS
words: was a little girl. And I know my mother used to tell about when my

speaker: CS
words: oldest sister was born, they lived back in Missouri, that Sundays

speaker: CS
words: she'd dress Mable all up and Dad'd take her down when he went down to

speaker: CS
words: get his mail, you know at the post office. And he was quite proud to

speaker: CS
words: take his little daughter down. But I know after my brother older than

speaker: CS
words: I, who was five years older than I, died I think my mother's health

speaker: CS
words: was never very good again. I think it just crushed her. He died in

speaker: CS
words: the Philippines. But then, you know, I think Dad- my oldest brother

speaker: CS
words: was over there working for the government, and Maurice was like

speaker: CS
words: a lot of kids I know, he wanted to get out. He didn't want to be tied

speaker: CS
words: down to school. He was only in prep school.

speaker: SS
words: When he went?

speaker: CS
words: Uh-huh. But my Dad let him go because Albert said he could come over

speaker: CS
words: to the Philippines and he'd see that he was okay. To Manila.

speaker: SS
words: You told Laura how he died. It sounds like that climate might have been

speaker: SS
words: really hard on him.

speaker: CS
words: Yes. Well, it was, because my oldest brother came home almost broke in

speaker: CS
words: health

speaker: SS
words: Did your mother- you mentioned to Laura the operation she had that was

speaker: SS
words: pretty serious and a coma. After that, did she still go out visiting?

speaker: CS
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: SS
words: She wasn't invalided then?

speaker: CS
words: No, no. She was active in church, but she just couldn't do all the

speaker: CS
words: things that a lot of women and mothers did. Her strength didn't war

speaker: CS
words: rant that.

speaker: SS
words: You mean the kind of things, like housecleaning?

speaker: CS
words: Yes, that sort of thing, that's what I mean. Physical work. She

speaker: CS
words: couldn't do that. But like her church work and so on, she could keep

speaker: CS
words: on with that, although she didn't go as much, I don't believe. It

speaker: CS
words: never seemed to affect her, she loved company. I know our ministers

speaker: CS
words: little daughters used to come out and she'd sit and play games with

speaker: CS
words: them. That was when I was too grownup to do that. And she did so much

speaker: CS
words: of that sort of thing.

speaker: SS
words: Was cleanliness really important in your family?

speaker: CS
words: Yes. My father was very fastidious. And before bathtub days when

speaker: CS
words: the bath was taken- sponge bath in a- we had a big bathpan that we

speaker: CS
words: used. And Sunday morning, Dad used that bathpan in the kitchen, after

speaker: CS
words: everybody'd gone to Sunday School. But, he'd go to church, you see,

speaker: CS
words: he'd get his toilet over. And I can remember so well when he used to

speaker: CS
words: shave. He'd have his towel that he'd spread on the dresser and put all

speaker: CS
words: his paraphenalia out; and then nothing was ever left our, he always

speaker: CS
words: cleaned up and put everything away. He was very fastidious. And, of

speaker: CS
words: course, in those days, men wore long underwear and so on ; it was such

speaker: CS
words: a trial to get things dry. But we had clothes racks and things and

speaker: CS
words: the clothes were hung on the porch. got dry.

speaker: SS
words: Did the girls do that after your mother was sick?

speaker: CS
words: Well, I'll tell you, there's a period in there I just don't quite rea

speaker: CS
words: lize how we got along. I know at one time, my sister older than I,

speaker: CS
words: the second one older than I, was teaching ^in the Moscow schools and I

speaker: CS
words: was in high school, I presume,- yes- and our clothes were sent out to

speaker: CS
words: the laundry. And where they were sent the husband must have smoked

speaker: CS
words: a pipe, because they'd come home just smelling so of tobacco 1 And

speaker: CS
words: then, of course, after that, we started sending all the flat work, the

speaker: CS
words: sheets, the towels and everything like that to the laundry. Mr. Green,

speaker: CS
words: by then his laundry was- of course, before that he did washing. But

speaker: CS
words: I don't think our washing ever went down there when it was done in wash

speaker: CS
words: tubs And-

speaker: SS
words: Was your father concerned, say, about the furniture not having any dust

speaker: SS
words: on it, and clean.

speaker: CS
words: Oh, yes. In his marble works, in the showroom, especially if he was

speaker: CS
words: showing a monoment or showing monuments to customers, out would come

speaker: CS
words: his handkerchief and all the time he was showing them, he'd be using

speaker: CS
words: his handkerchief to wipe them off. But his method of dusting was a

speaker: CS
words: feather duster, and of course, the dust settled and there was a lot

speaker: CS
words: of marble dust in the shop from the carving. But, oh, he was very—

speaker: CS
words: no dust, no anything around. In fact, in our house every morning,

speaker: CS
words: even the cleaning days- there was a cleaning day once a week, and

speaker: CS
words: everything was, oh, boy, and they used to sweep and. dust would roll

speaker: CS
words: out, and we always hoped for a wind from the east on sweeping day, so

speaker: CS
words: it would all go out the front doorl (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: Did everybody take part in that?

speaker: CS
words: No. No. I don't remember my mother ever sweeping, but it would be my

speaker: CS
words: older sisters and after they were away from home, if we didn't have

speaker: CS
words: help, I'd do it. And I remember my mother thought it was pretty heavy

speaker: CS
words: work for me, and she would do so many little things for my benefit,

speaker: CS
words: because I had done all that hard work. And I can remember one of our

speaker: CS
words: bedrooms upstairs, before had the bathroom had a stove in it, and

speaker: CS
words: that's where the girls always took their baths. And I can remember

speaker: CS
words: on Saturday, when I was growing up, after the girls were away from

speaker: CS
words: home teaching, my sisters, after I'd clean the house I'd have my bath

speaker: CS
words: and go to someone's house or go do something, go to town, or do some

speaker: CS
words: thing, and my mother would empty my bathwater and do all that, because

speaker: CS
words: I'd worked hard in the morning.

speaker: SS
words: Well, the work you had to do in the sweeping: you had to lift every

speaker: SS
words: thing up?

speaker: CS
words: Oh, yes. The way our house was; the master bedroom was off of the

speaker: CS
words: living room and the chairs were all carted in there while the dining

speaker: CS
words: room and the living room were swept, because the dust would just enve

speaker: CS
words: lope you. But every morning the thing was around the heater in the

speaker: CS
words: living room- you took a little whisk broom and a dust pan and

speaker: CS
words: cleaned all that up, so that.general cleaning didn't go on only once

speaker: CS
words: a week.

speaker: SS
words: You didn't have rugs on the floors then?

speaker: CS
words: Oh, yes. Carpets; wall-to-wall carpets.

speaker: SS
words: So they have to be swept.

speaker: CS
words: Yes, but I don't think, probably they got as dirty as lots of people's

speaker: CS
words: carpets did because we all came in the kitchen door and left our rub

speaker: CS
words: bers or boots or so on( And my father was the kind, he never

speaker: CS
words: went outside without wiping his feet on the rug at the back door when

speaker: CS
words: he came in. So I don't think the cleaning was as hard, maybe as it

speaker: CS
words: was in some other homes that had to clean that way.

speaker: SS
words: You know, you mentioned eating in the dining room.

speaker: CS
words: Uh-huh. Three meals a day.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that that's what most Moscow families did at that time?

speaker: SS
words: Because I know in the country they always ate in the kitchen.

speaker: CS
words: ' Well, I really can't say. Some of my- Well, I can think of two friendsone

speaker: CS
words: friend, her father was a doctor, they always ate in the dining room.

speaker: CS
words: And they usually had a hired girl. Another friend, her father was a

speaker: CS
words: blacksmith, and they always ate in the kitchen because he'd come from

speaker: CS
words: the shop and he'd wash up and they'd eat in the kitchen. I don't re

speaker: CS
words: member of ever eating in the dining room at their house; ever.

speaker: SS
words: Well what made it nicer for you to eat in the kitchen? Because you

speaker: SS
words: said you really liked it in the

speaker: CS
words: Oh, I don't know.

speaker: SS
words: Just cozier?

speaker: CS
words: Yes. Some way.

speaker: SS
words: Smaller and closer.

speaker: CS
words: Well, our kitchen was big, but it just seemed to me that- well, maybe

speaker: CS
words: just because it was different. But I just thought that was— and it

speaker: CS
words: wasn't breakfast, it'd be Sunday evening supper. And even after I was

speaker: CS
words: married and my sister was keeping house for my father, they never ate

speaker: CS
words: out on a table in the kitchen. There was a table there that they

speaker: CS
words: could have eaten on. There was this worktable with^bins below it and

speaker: CS
words: so on, and there was another table that sat over by the other kitchen

speaker: CS
words: door where the groceries were put when they were delivered and so on,

speaker: CS
words: but that was kept clear, they could have eaten there. But they just

speaker: CS
words: never did it. Meals were always served in the dining room.

speaker: SS
words: So, really the only use for the kitchen was for pretty much to prepare

speaker: SS
words: meals.

speaker: CS
words: Yes, and the laundry.

speaker: SS
words: And the laundry. Did you visit at all in the kitchen, if you had time?

speaker: CS
words: Only if for some reason we ate out there Sunday evening and sat around

speaker: CS
words: the opened the oven door and sat around there,

speaker: SS
words: "So usually was it in the living room or dining room, either one?

speaker: CS
words: Always. But not so much around the table, maybe, as we did in the li

speaker: CS
words: ving room. We just went to the living room. And it wasn't a parlor,

speaker: CS
words: it was a living room. Front room, we called it. But it wasn't in any

speaker: CS
words: sense a parlor.

speaker: SS
words: What would the difference be between your living room and a parlor?

speaker: CS
words: Well, now some friends of mine- the blinds were kept pulled and there

speaker: CS
words: was no heat in there only when they knew they were going to use the

speaker: CS
words: parlor. And it was just a dressed up room. They did have nice things

speaker: CS
words: in there. But, I know when I used to visit that friend I stayed all

speaker: CS
words: night there quite a bit when I was a kid, we'd go in there once in a

speaker: CS
words: while and just kind of look around, with a little awe, maybe.

speaker: SS
words: And seldom used.

speaker: CS
words: Seldom used.

speaker: SS
words: But in your living room in your family, what did you have? Was it

speaker: SS
words: mostly chairs? Was there a sofa?

speaker: CS
words: Oh, no, the library table that Harry has in there, and rockers, we had

speaker: CS
words: rockers and piano and a couch. We called it a lounge, kind of like

speaker: CS
words: thatthing at the museum. But our furniture wasn t grand, but

speaker: CS
words: it was very comfortable and never chosen as a set or anything. Every

speaker: CS
words: piece had just been bought as my father could afford to buy it. And

speaker: CS
words: then sometimes we got a new chair and the old rocking chair was put up

speaker: CS
words: stairs in somebody's room.

speaker: SS
words: In your family, did your father show affection to the kids much, or

speaker: SS
words: was that considered—

speaker: CS
words: Oh, no, uh-huh. I don't think there was ever a night when I was a

speaker: CS
words: kid or my father was too tired that I didn't climb in his lap while he

speaker: CS
words: read his paper, tired as he was. And his favorite word for calling us

speaker: CS
words: was "Darling."

speaker: SS
words: That's really nice. Because I have an idea in some families parents

speaker: SS
words: didn't feel that they ought to show affection for the kids.

speaker: CS
words: It wasn't so in our family.

speaker: SS
words: Did you do much to please your father? I mean, when you were a kid

speaker: SS
words: did you feel motivated by that?

speaker: CS
words: Well, I think I probably told Laura that after my brother, Maurice was

speaker: CS
words: away from home and he did the sprinkling and mowing the lawn and so on,

speaker: CS
words: I fell heir to that job because - that's why I never learned to cook

speaker: CS
words: because I did those things. But I know I used to be in a hurry to get

speaker: CS
words: off someplace or something and I didn't do very good sprinkling, but

speaker: CS
words: I made it look awful wet on the surface so Dad'd think I'd done a good

speaker: CS
words: job of sprinkling. But I don't think I ever thought much about whether

speaker: CS
words: I was pleasing my father, only in little things like that. But, I'm

speaker: CS
words: sure I didn't try to displease him at all. And from the time I was,

speaker: CS
words: well- when my sisters were away from home teaching and my mother couldn't

speaker: CS
words: go downtown. shop and so on, I was just put on my own to buy my own

speaker: CS
words: clothes. And I was never told, "You can't get this or you can't get

speaker: CS
words: that." I knew about what I was expected to spend and I stayed within

speaker: CS
words: bounds when I was a teenager with my clothes. I remember one time^I

speaker: CS
words: got a coat and oh, I got so sick of it! Because we usually wore o ur

speaker: CS
words: coats two years, our winter coats. But I^chose^it, it was what I wan

speaker: CS
words: ted, and nothing was said at the time that maybe I'd get tired of it

speaker: CS
words: but I surely did. I remember so well when I was a little girl; real

speaker: CS
words: little, and the family pocketbook wasn't so big, my mother took me

speaker: CS
words: down to Creighton's store and I was going to have a new winter coat.

speaker: CS
words: And Mr. Creighton, I think he was very fond of my mother, and he came

speaker: CS
words: up while they were showing us and this little red coat, I can just

speaker: CS
words: still see it- pretty red coat and it had a velvet collar. Oh, I

speaker: CS
words: thought it was so beautiful, and my thought it was really more

speaker: CS
words: than she could pay, and as I remember, it just about fit me then and

speaker: CS
words: there wasn't any room for growth for the next year, you know, and Mr

speaker: CS
words: Creighton came long and said, "She looks pretty nice in that, and I

speaker: CS
words: think she should have it." And, anyway, Mama decided to buy the red

speaker: CS
words: coat. And I don't remember how long I wore it or anything, but I can

speaker: CS
words: just still see that red collar with the red velvet collar.

speaker: SS
words: He was pretty kind- I mean Creighton.

speaker: CS
words: Oh, yes. We always bought— of course, eating in the dining room ofj.

speaker: CS
words: white tableclothes, they wore out, you know, and every year thatthe

speaker: CS
words: red tag sale that they had it in those days, too, Mama would go

speaker: CS
words: in and buy a certain number of sheets and tablecloths, you know, for

speaker: CS
words: the year. And Mr. Creighton went to Ireland every year and bought

speaker: CS
words: linen. A lot of my linen- it was all from Creighton's store, was

speaker: CS
words: in my cedar chest. And he just delighted showing mama the linen he

speaker: CS
words: got in Ireland, you know. And then every Christmas he always sent her

speaker: CS
words: a great big box of candy. And we always thought that was nice.

speaker: SS
words: I wanted to ask you some about the way that more prominent families

speaker: SS
words: were in Moscow. The Creightons probably would be considered one of

speaker: SS
words: them.

speaker: CS
words: Well, yes, in a way, but she never mixed, Mrs. Creighton. And some

speaker: CS
words: of her friends that she'd go to see were some of the most humble people

speaker: CS
words: in Moscow. The shoemaker's wife and the neighbors beyond us, we kind

speaker: CS
words: of—looked-on them as white trash, because they were from down South

speaker: CS
words: someplace and they weren't much for keeping their yard clean and so on.

speaker: CS
words: They were good people but Mrs. Creighton would go and call on them. It

speaker: CS
words: was strange some of her friendships. And I don't know that in the

speaker: CS
words: society of Moscow then, I don't think Mrs. Creighton functioned in

speaker: CS
words: that at all.

speaker: SS
words: Mr. Creighton, was he the same way? Did he mix with ordinary people?

speaker: CS
words: Not any more than just in the store. I think they were very- maybe

speaker: CS
words: I shouldn't say- but when Harry was a kid and he worked for C reightons

speaker: CS
words: delivering packages, and he carried their laundry down to their wash

speaker: CS
words: woman, that was one of his jobs. And Mrs. Creighton was always awfully

speaker: CS
words: good to him, and he'd go and maybe she'd always have him come in; she'd

speaker: CS
words: have a treat for him. Any maybe he'd have his hands in pockets and

speaker: CS
words: she'd tell him, "Take your hands out of your pockets." And she'd tell

speaker: CS
words: him things like that. stand with his hands in his pockets. And

speaker: CS
words: his hands got rough and she told him how he could avoid those rough

speaker: CS
words: hands was to soap his hands real good and have someone sprinkle a tea

speaker: CS
words: spoon of sugar in 'em and rub that in your hands, no foiling. And

speaker: CS
words: then in a day or two he'd go back down and get the clean laundry and

speaker: CS
words: take it back to Creightons.

speaker: SS
words: Did you know her?

speaker: CS
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: SS
words: Did you ever have an idea of why she mixed with ordinary people?

speaker: CS
words: No, I never did.

speaker: SS
words: It's very unusual.

speaker: CS
words: Well, she'd stop at our house lots of times,; she liked my mother.

speaker: SS
words: Sounds like she was more interested in people as people than society.

speaker: CS
words: Probably, I don't know. But I can see her, she was quite big, and she

speaker: CS
words: wore a beaver coat; beautiful beaver coat, and she walked quite proud.

speaker: CS
words: I can still see her when she'd go by to go up the street to the neigh

speaker: CS
words: bors. But as far as mixing in church or social life, I don't believe

speaker: CS
words: they did.

speaker: SS
words: If you were going to think of some of some of the leading families in

speaker: SS
words: Moscow at the time in social life and church affairs, who would some

speaker: SS
words: of them be?

speaker: CS
words: Well, of course, the David family was always prominent and the

speaker: CS
words: Smiths, and Charlie Shields, he was the hardware man. These people

speaker: CS
words: just happened to be in our church. And the Watkins, Carol Ryrie Brink's

speaker: CS
words: grandparents, but, of course, I don't think there were many clubs or

speaker: CS
words: anything. There was a club up on the— that was called the High U Club,

speaker: CS
words: sort of a sewing social club, that was up near the old David home, in

speaker: CS
words: that neighborhood. And then, in our neighborhood a woman came in, I

speaker: CS
words: don't know how old I was, I was just a little girl and they had a lit

speaker: CS
words: tle girl; we played together. And she started a neighborhood sewing

speaker: CS
words: club, and that was about the extent of it. But you would have maybe

speaker: CS
words: two or three couples come in in the evening to your homeland play cards.

speaker: SS
words: Who were their friends?

speaker: CS
words: My parents?

speaker: SS
words: Yes, the people that they would have associate with them? Socially,

speaker: SS
words: like that?

speaker: CS
words: Well, they were mostly, I think, people that lived quite near in the

speaker: CS
words: neighborhood. And I can remember the Naylors used to come and play

speaker: CS
words: cards. And, oh, I don't remember; I remember one time my mother and

speaker: CS
words: father were having people in to play cards in the evening and several

speaker: CS
words: of my friends and I, we always thought when Friday night came we had

speaker: CS
words: to do something, you know, because the next day was Saturday and so

speaker: CS
words: on. And we'd go to each other's houses- and I remember that time we

speaker: CS
words: played games and things and have little refreshments, you know. And

speaker: CS
words: that time I'd invited my friends and probably hadn't consulted my mother

speaker: CS
words: and so the living room was in use, so we- I had my friends in the kit

speaker: CS
words: chen. But we played games in the kitchen. They were all little girls.

speaker: CS
words: But that was one thing; we never had to ask if we were going to a p icnic

speaker: CS
words: or something.they told so-and-so a cake, or sandwiches or so on,

speaker: CS
words: we could just always say yes, we could do that, but some of the child

speaker: CS
words: ren had to go home and ask first. And I always thought that was so

speaker: CS
words: funny. As I got older, sometimes I made the cakes myself and they

speaker: CS
words: weren't too good, but I never had to ask if I could take a cake to the

speaker: CS
words: picnic.

speaker: SS
words: What do you think that difference was? Do you think your parents just

speaker: SS
words: wanted you kids to do whatever you wanted to?

speaker: CS
words: Well, not do whatever you wanted to, especially, but they wanted us to

speaker: CS
words: feel that we had the privilege of- if we wanted to say we could take

speaker: CS
words: something, we could take it.

speaker: SS
words: That's really what it was.

speaker: CS
words: Yes. We didn't have to go home and say, could I take a cake to the

speaker: CS
words: picnic Saturday? We just knew we could.

speaker: SS
words: In that way, they respected your wishes as people.

speaker: CS
words: I think so, very much.

speaker: SS
words: Even though you were still kids.

speaker: CS
words: Uh-huh. I think so, very much.

speaker: SS
words: That sounds like a rather progressive attitude towards kids, too.

speaker: CS
words: Well, my father was not that way, I was told, with my older brothers

speaker: CS
words: and sisters, even when they were in college. went to parties and

speaker: CS
words: dances at the University and they were told so be home at such-and-

speaker: CS
words: - such a time and they had to be home then.

speaker: SS
words: But it was different wiht you?

speaker: CS
words: Yes, I knew I wasn't expected, and.Maurice too, we knew we weren't ex

speaker: CS
words: pected to stay out too late, but we never had to leave the party be

speaker: CS
words: fore it was over. So, I think my father was more tolerant with Mau

speaker: CS
words: rice and I.

speaker: SS
words: You know these parties you mentioned to Laura, the QAE Club and some

speaker: SS
words: of these parties you went to as a teenager, too. What were the acti

speaker: SS
words: vities that the young people would engage in? Would t hey be party

speaker: SS
words: games?

speaker: CS
words: Yes, party games.

speaker: SS
words: Like—?

speaker: CS
words: Spin the platter, and wink 'em and such games as that. The whole group

speaker: CS
words: would participate in.

speaker: SS
words: Would there be the sorts of things- some of the things that they dld

speaker: SS
words: like Happy is a Miller Boy?

speaker: CS
words: Well, not at those parties. We did that when I was in high school. We

speaker: CS
words: had parties; the old building was torn down, and we'd do that in the

speaker: CS
words: hall, and we'd get in the front hall and Happy Is the Miller Boy came

speaker: CS
words: back to me when I read Lola's book. Because we did that and Skip-tomy-

speaker: CS
words: Lou.

speaker: SS
words: But those would be in school not in the home?

speaker: CS
words: Not in the home.

speaker: SS
words: At home it would be-

speaker: CS
words: Just parlor games. I don't think we sat at tables and played games like

speaker: CS
words: children play, it was always just group. jf They'd put out a whole lot

speaker: CS
words: of objects and you'd go look at them and then write down all you could

speaker: CS
words: remember, that sort of thing, too, you know.

speaker: SS
words: Dancing, though, was that done in the homes?

speaker: CS
words: Course, too many homes weren t - our home was^a place wh ere we could

speaker: CS
words: have danced, only we just didn't dance. We just played games until I

speaker: CS
words: mm.

speaker: CS
words: got in- well, I can tell you about one experience dancing. We were

speaker: CS
words: having- in church there were two classes, a girls' class and a

speaker: CS
words: boys' class-

speaker: SS
words: Is this the story about how you sneaked back?

speaker: CS
words: Yes, Laura had that. I was thinking I had told her.

speaker: SS
words: I was wondering about that- when you snuck back that was because the

speaker: SS
words: Sunday School teacher wouldn't have allowed you to dance?

speaker: CS
words: No, he wasn't that way at all, he would have gone along and probably

speaker: CS
words: sat and played the piano, but I don't know, we just thought that was

speaker: CS
words: fun to do it that way, or something, I don't know why we did that.

speaker: SS
words: You just didn't want to let him and the other teacher know.

speaker: CS
words: Well, that was Doctor " .He was such a good sport. There was

speaker: CS
words: a great thing then to- he'd play for us, and this group of girls, t eenagers

speaker: CS
words: would stand around the piano and sing all the popular songs, you

speaker: CS
words: know. And he just entered right in that. He'd sit and play the

speaker: CS
words: piano just as long as we wanted to sing.

speaker: SS
words: So he wouldn't have objected to dancing?

speaker: CS
words: I don't think he would have. I know he wouldn't have later, when he had

speaker: CS
words: his own children.

speaker: SS
words: Well, that makes it seem like you were really playing a prank.

speaker: CS
words: I think we thought it was a prank, and I don't know why we thought we

speaker: CS
words: had to do it that way.

speaker: SS
words: Then the girl got the people next door to chaperone you?

speaker: SS
words: SS Uh-huh. The young woman. Miss Madeline Shields and she brought her

speaker: SS
words: fiancee, and they chaperoned the party.

speaker: SS
words: Was this girl Mrs. Day's niece?

speaker: CS
words: That had the party? Uh-huh. And Mrs. Day was not at home at the time.

speaker: CS
words: But she knew that the party was to be there- well, that wasn't on the

speaker: CS
words: sly.

speaker: SS
words: To you kids, was going to the Day's house a big deal? Was it some

speaker: SS
words: thing special?

speaker: CS
words: Yes, we thought that was something special. Especially if you were

speaker: CS
words: asked eat there. I don't remember whether they had more than

speaker: CS
words: just a hired girl in the kitchen or what, but you were served at the

speaker: CS
words: dining room table, and most of us weren't used to that, you see. Our

speaker: CS
words: meals were all served family style. I don't even remember my father

speaker: CS
words: ever serving the plates too much, it was just- only he used to f ix my

speaker: CS
words: food when I was little, especially my pancakes. And I didn't want any

speaker: CS
words: body else to fix 'em but my father. And I just wonder if they'd still

speaker: CS
words: taste that good to me! He'd put a lot of butter then sprinkle sugar

speaker: CS
words: and then pour cream on 'eml They'd puff up, you know. Oh, I thought

speaker: CS
words: that was delicious.

speaker: SS
words: That was special for you?

speaker: CS
words: Uh-huh. You know, not long ago, Miss is an artist, you know, and Mr. Westerlund,

speaker: CS
words: they were out at our place to a party, a talking party, and

speaker: CS
words: we got to talking about people painting and so on, and I told 'em that

speaker: CS
words: I just didn't have a bit of that in me. I said, "I remember the only

speaker: CS
words: picture I painted was a watercolor, and it was a black sailboat on water."

speaker: CS
words: And right away Mr. Westerlund said that showed I was an unhappy child,

speaker: CS
words: and he said, "Do you like the water?" And I said, "No." And that I

speaker: CS
words: disliked the water and so on. And since then, I've thought about that

speaker: CS
words: so much, and I said that wasn't true, sounded like my father

speaker: CS
words: domineered me, that painting. And I said, "That wasn't true." And

speaker: CS
words: since then I've thought, everybody in the class, the teacher told them

speaker: CS
words: to paint that.

speaker: SS
words: A black sailboat on water?

speaker: CS
words: Uh-huh.

speaker: SS
words: Well, I certainly don't see how you can conclude that— from a paint

speaker: SS
words: ing.

speaker: CS
words: Well, I don't like water, and I don't enjoy boats too much, but I don't

speaker: CS
words: know how he could say that that showed my father was—

speaker: SS
words: Sounds like armchair psychology, to me.

speaker: CS
words: Well, I don't know psychology, anyway. But, I think the

speaker: CS
words: next time I see him I'm going to bring that to his mind and tell him

speaker: CS
words: that,the whole class did the same thing.

speaker: SS
words: Maybe it was the teacher who was dominated by her father!

speaker: CS
words: Yes, that's right. I thought I might come across that painting in all

speaker: CS
words: that junk I had, but I didn't. I was in hopes I would so I could show

speaker: CS
words: it to Miss ' and Mr. Westerlund.

speaker: SS
words: You know, talking about the people who were well-to-do in Moscow I'm

speaker: SS
words: wondering about the extent, if any, of social distance that other peo

speaker: SS
words: ple felt. For instance, did your family feel a social distance between

speaker: SS
words: you and the Shields, say?

speaker: CS
words: No, no. I don't think that was true. In those days, they did have a

speaker: CS
words: Historical Club, I spoke of. His three daughters were all quite so-

speaker: CS
words: cially involved in, and he was and crude in speech, but they were

speaker: CS
words: about the best dressed girls in Moscow and he was the same kind of a

speaker: CS
words: father about wanting his family. And, I know Marjorie- there was a

speaker: CS
words: little group of us and we got out on honor if you made certain grades,

speaker: CS
words: you didn't have to take the final examinations. There was a system in

speaker: CS
words: the Moscow schools. We called it getting out on honor. And he always

speaker: CS
words: gave her five dollars when she got out on honor. And so, she wouldwe'd

speaker: CS
words: all go and buy food, stuff we liked, bought from the store, you

speaker: CS
words: know. Dill pickles like they had in the store, not like we had at home,

speaker: CS
words: and ice cream, and we'd buy such stuff and always had it at their

speaker: CS
words: house in the kitchen. But she always got five dollars; none of the

speaker: CS
words: rest of us did that.

speaker: SS
words: When she got her five dollars, you'd all go out and spend the money

speaker: SS
words: together? That was very nice of her.

speaker: CS
words: Uh-huh.

speaker: SS
words: Was this

speaker: CS
words: Uh-huh.

speaker: SS
words: Colorful fellow.

speaker: CS
words: Uh-huh. Then I had another friend, Napa Naylor and right there where

speaker: CS
words: our house was on Third Street, there was a little corner grocery, a

speaker: CS
words: widow started it and she did sewing in her store, and maybe I'd have

speaker: CS
words: a nickle to spend, but Nepa always had a dime, and so we could buy

speaker: CS
words: more penny candy, but she shared right along with me, because she had

speaker: CS
words: a dime and I only had a nickle.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that the kids moved in the same circles,that the parents

speaker: SS
words: would move in somewhat different circles? Depending on their wealth?

speaker: CS
words: No, I don't think it depended on the wealth or anything, but people

speaker: CS
words: did things more as neighborhoods. I think the social evolved around

speaker: CS
words: the neighborhood; the social life. And as far now the Shields, probably

speaker: CS
words: had as much money, I'm sure, as anyone, and there were the two

speaker: CS
words: boys that were in the family that were in my group. But Mrs. Shields

speaker: CS
words: was a very— I don't think she ever entered into any social life. She

speaker: CS
words: was just a quiet type of woman. Little woman that stayed home and

speaker: CS
words: ran her house. No, I don't think the money had anything - or the amount

speaker: CS
words: of wealth, or whatever you want to call it the family had.

speaker: SS
words: Well, what did determine then, social—

speaker: CS
words: I just don't know.

speaker: SS
words: What would make a family, or an individual that was—

speaker: CS
words: I really don't know, because I can think of some who didn't have very

speaker: CS
words: much who were just- I just don't know what- I don't think there was—

speaker: CS
words: the amount of what the family had had anything to do with what group

speaker: CS
words: or circle they mixed in. I don't believe it did.

speaker: SS
words: It sounds like the church was a very essential thing.

speaker: CS
words: It was very much socially. And, of course, when my brothers and sis

speaker: CS
words: ters; my older brothers and sisters, were young people the church was

speaker: CS
words: quite the center. They had an organization, Christian Endeavor, and

speaker: CS
words: that was much of the social life for the young people who belonged to

speaker: CS
words: the Presbyterian Church.

speaker: SS
words: It was less so by the time that you were growing?

speaker: CS
words: Yes, uh-huh. That's right. Except for like these Sunday School par

speaker: CS
words: ties we threw on our own, you know.

speaker: SS
words: Had the kind of parties; this QAE Club and that sort of thing- was

speaker: SS
words: that sort of taking the place of the church's—

speaker: CS
words: Probably. Because I know when I came along I didn't want any of that

speaker: CS
words: Christian Endeavor stuff and so on. They tried to keep it going in

speaker: CS
words: our church, but it just died out, that didn't appeal to me.

speaker: SS
words: Why not?

speaker: CS
words: I don't know. Maybe because we'd evolved our own social life this

speaker: CS
words: other way.

speaker: SS
words: You mentioned a Missionary Society to Laura, and that they combined.

speaker: SS
words: Which was a good move.

speaker: CS
words: Uh-huh.

speaker: SS
words: But was the Missionary Society separate in what it was trying to do?

speaker: SS
words: Was it trying to raise money for the foreign missions?

speaker: CS
words: No, not especially, so the people would be informed. They sent money

speaker: CS
words: to the Church Board to go for foreign missions and home missions, they

speaker: CS
words: called it, and so on. But there weren't too many women- many of the

speaker: CS
words: women in the church, like the Ladies' Aid Society, because even though

speaker: CS
words: they always had a religious type of service and everything, it was

speaker: CS
words: more social, you see, and they did the things like the ice cream soc

speaker: CS
words: ials and things to raise money to get some new dishes and so on.

speaker: SS
words: Well what did the Missionary Society do?

speaker: CS
words: Well, of course,the women studied the different missions that we helped

speaker: CS
words: support, you know, but Mrs. Gormley thought that was all wrong, so she-

speaker: SS
words: So it wasn't as much fun for the women then?

speaker: CS
words: No, the in the Missionary Society, they were more serious about

speaker: CS
words: what they were doing. It just didn't involve so many women. So she

speaker: CS
words: was pretty smart because that way, it put some of the missionary work

speaker: CS
words: into the Ladies' Aid Society.

speaker: SS
words: Would it be true to say that the women were- the people who were pri

speaker: SS
words: marily involved in the church activities and that the men were much

speaker: SS
words: less so? Now, I mean outside of just the Sunday service? What I'm

speaker: SS
words: wondering, is whether the women were the people that kept the church

speaker: SS
words: going?

speaker: CS
words: Oh, yes, yes, absolutely. Then later, I think- well, probably mini

speaker: CS
words: sters came who tried to have men's groups. But they never got across

speaker: CS
words: and someway, you know, they'd die out. They'd go good for a while,

speaker: CS
words: but then when they started having midweek- like with a potluck supper

speaker: CS
words: and men and women both, why then, men would come to that, you see.

speaker: CS
words: Now they're trying to start,

speaker: CS
words: but they put it so early because if the professors come they have to

speaker: CS
words: be free to get to school, the six-forty-five breakfast. Harry wentthe

speaker: CS
words: other one they had but he didn't even read it on the Sunday bul

speaker: CS
words: letin, I think they were going to have that, but he said he wouldn't

speaker: CS
words: be going, he didn't think after the last one, because it's just a con

speaker: CS
words: tinental breakfast and that wouldn't bother him, but- In fact, I think

speaker: CS
words: it's just kind of bull session or that sort of thing. But they've been

speaker: CS
words: trying to get that started in our church with the men again.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think it would be fair to say that the church in the early days,

speaker: SS
words: the church you belonged to, really determined to a large extent of who

speaker: SS
words: you would spend your social life with?

speaker: CS
words: Perhaps.

speaker: SS
words: Maybe that combined with the neighborhood that you lived in?

speaker: CS
words: Uh-huh. Of course, as I remember our neighborhood, we lived in what

speaker: CS
words: they called Swede town, they were all Lutherans and not many Metho

speaker: CS
words: dists, so they didn't involve what your religion was, you know, in

speaker: CS
words: your neighborhood. 'Course now, in those days, men went to lodges.

speaker: SS
words: They did?

speaker: CS
words: Uh-huh. My father was an Odd Fellow. He was a Woodman of the World.

speaker: CS
words: I think he felt that if he was a member that he should go to those things.

speaker: CS
words: And most of them had ladies auxilaries and then they'd get together for

speaker: CS
words: suppers and parties on special occasions. But I think lodges played a

speaker: CS
words: big part in the men's social life then. I am sure they did.

speaker: SS
words: Was your mother in the ladies' auxiliaries? Of the groups that he was in?

speaker: CS
words: Yes.I know were Woodmen of the World and the

speaker: CS
words: the Wo men of the Woodcraft were the women. And I was ta ken as a child

speaker: CS
words: by my parents to parties at Alma Reeling's  because they had one

speaker: CS
words: of the big homes. Now as far as Alma ever entering into any of this

speaker: CS
words: I never remember her at all. Course Alma's- I shouldn't say this- has

speaker: CS
words: always been a kind of a loner. She is that type. But when we were in

speaker: CS
words: high school then her brother was in my class, and Mrs.

speaker: CS
words: used to have parties for us quite often.

speaker: SS
words: Connected wiht the lodge?

speaker: CS
words: No. No. Just Ralph's class. School parties. In fact, I remember

speaker: CS
words: after we'd been out of high school a year. A Ralph had a party when we

speaker: CS
words: came home, and she'd just gone to so much trouble. I remember one

speaker: CS
words: thing- it was supper I think she had us to- and she made mashed sweet

speaker: CS
words: potatoes- she was quite astistic. Were you ever in Alma's home?

speaker: SS
words: Never inside.

speaker: CS
words: Well, Mrs. oh, she'd make- I remember one thing she had was a

speaker: CS
words: section of a log with the bark on it, and she'd varnished it and had

speaker: CS
words: a painting in it, you know, that sort of thing. She was great on

speaker: CS
words: making something like that. But for this party she'd molded these littie

speaker: CS
words: animals someway of this mashed sweet potato. I don't remember what

speaker: CS
words: we had for the rest of the supper, but I just remember those little

speaker: CS
words: things she had formed and put in the oven, you know, heated up with

speaker: CS
words: butter.

speaker: SS
words: At that would you have played games, or would you have danced?

speaker: CS
words: No, we didn't dance because there were so many more girls than there

speaker: CS
words: were boys. But of course, at the school parties, part of the girls

speaker: CS
words: would have to take- be the partner, you see, because in my class therey

speaker: CS
words: were six boys and fourteen girls in high school.- So when we'd march

speaker: CS
words: around with the Miller Boy, part of the girls would have to take the

speaker: CS
words: gent's part. Ladies step forward and the gents fall back.

speaker: SS
words: So like at the Reeling's house it would have been- talking?

speaker: CS
words: Yes, and singing. We used to like to sing so much when we were kids.

speaker: CS
words: There was a lot of that. And I can remember when my olderTand my sis

speaker: CS
words: ters- that was a lot of when the young people'd get together.

speaker: CS
words: - play and everybody'd sing. The Ramstadt family, they were in our

speaker: CS
words: neighborhood. They were all good singers. And Mrs. Oberg, Ellen

speaker: CS
words: Ramstadt used to play the piano for singing. And that was quite the

speaker: CS
words: thing to sing around the piano.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know whether your father felt close to his lodge brothers and

speaker: SS
words: that he would help them if they needed help?

speaker: CS
words: Oh, yes, I think there was that feeling.

speaker: SS
words: It would be a special feeling that you wouldn't just have for anybody?

speaker: CS
words: I think so. I think so. And I think maybe the Odd Fellows and some

speaker: CS
words: of those things are like that today. Now, I think a bigger lodge like

speaker: CS
words: the Elks and the Moose and so on; I don't know what they're like. But

speaker: CS
words: I think the Odd Fellows are still that kind of a group in Moscow. It's

speaker: CS
words: smaller.

speaker: SS
words: Did Swedetown have a character of it's own at that time? They call

speaker: SS
words: it Swedetown and I wonder- was it really it's own community then?

speaker: CS
words: No, it wasn't. There were a lot of Scandinavian people lived there,

speaker: CS
words: but there were a lot of others too. It just happened that the majority

speaker: CS
words: who had homes in that part of the town were Scandinavian. But; Chara

speaker: CS
words: cters- well, of course, farther down our street Lynn Avenue,

speaker: CS
words: was down there toward the bridge, he was the character. Then we had a

speaker: CS
words: Negro family who lived down that way, the Mc Crays. And, of course, I

speaker: CS
words: think Jimmy Mc Cray was in my room for a while at school. And my brother

speaker: CS
words: would always tease me, my oldest brother, about Jimmy Mc Cray, you know.

speaker: CS
words: Of course, I'd get very upset when he did that.

speaker: SS
words: Tease you how? That you were-

speaker: CS
words: Jimmy's girlfriend or something. And I don't ever remember of even

speaker: CS
words: walking along with Jimmy going to school or anything. That was just a

speaker: CS
words: way to kid me.

speaker: SS
words: When you kids were in the same class, do you think that the kids real-

speaker: CS
words: that he was a different color from the others?

speaker: CS
words: Well, yes, but he wasn't as I remember, Jimmy wasn't in school very

speaker: CS
words: long. But, as I remember, they were never pushed aside or anything.

speaker: SS
words: Well, what I was thinking, whether there was a different fefcling about

speaker: SS
words: that area than say, in other places.

speaker: CS
words: No. No.

speaker: SS
words: Was it any better or worse off financially than other parts of town?

speaker: CS
words: No. Many of those Scandinavian men weren't professional men or any

speaker: CS
words: thing, but their children went to college and everything. I don't

speaker: CS
words: know- now Allen Ramstad's grandfather, they had a big place and I know

speaker: CS
words: he used to sell cherries and he'd lay carpets, and whether he made all

speaker: CS
words: of his living that way, I don't know. But his sons all went on to do

speaker: CS
words: well. And I don't know whether they were all college graduates or not

speaker: CS
words: but they had the opportunity school. Course, now the Naylor family

speaker: CS
words: that I speak of they had big farms out here, north of town, and they

speaker: CS
words: still own them. But they didn't go on after high school to college.

speaker: CS
words: They just started farming; the boys. And they had money, too. He was

speaker: CS
words: one of the wealthiest men in the county, Mr. Naylor.

speaker: SS
words: Does that men that a lot of people just didn't value going to colleges

speaker: SS
words: at that time?

speaker: CS
words: Well, I don't know how it was. My father wanted all of his children

speaker: CS
words: to go to college. But evidently the Naylors didn't feel that.

speaker: SS
words: Of course, Farming didn't traditionally didn't require that.

speaker: CS
words: No, and they had begun to acquire quite a lot of land. And Mr. Naylor

speaker: CS
words: sold insurance and he was a great one to sit in the office and put his

speaker: CS
words: feet up and talk to people, you know. In the early days he was sheriff

speaker: CS
words: - and they were prominent in the county and they were prominent in the

speaker: CS
words: Rebeccas and Odd Fellows. And in those days they had the GAR, Grand

speaker: CS
words: Army of the Republic. They were prominent in that. They were early

speaker: CS
words: day settlers here. But they didn't move in what you'd call in the

speaker: CS
words: better circles, although they were always accepted and so on.

speaker: SS
words: Would the better circles, the best circles be those which would have-

speaker: SS
words: The Days and Mc Connels and those people in them? No, you said the

speaker: SS
words: Shields didn't.

speaker: CS
words: Mrs. Shields did not have any social ideas.

speaker: SS
words: I am thinkg of who the better circle would be.

speaker: CS
words: Well, that's kind of hard for me to say, because I don't know, they

speaker: CS
words: kind of thought of up around the Day home and farther east the David

speaker: CS
words: home around the Catholic Church there-rthat was kind of Nob Hill or not.

speaker: CS
words: I don't know whether they thought of it that way.

speaker: SS
words: Well, who lived there besides the Davids and the Days right in that

speaker: SS
words: area?

speaker: CS
words: The Smiths, and the Is the doctor I spoke of. His daughter

speaker: CS
words: was one of my best friends. But still, right in that same block lived

speaker: CS
words: the harness maker, Webber. See, there was no distinction, and I think

speaker: CS
words: as far as groups were concerned, it was just more within the neighbor

speaker: CS
words: hood group of congenial people who would get together. And then the

speaker: CS
words: lodges, and I think many of those people didn't enter into that lodge

speaker: CS
words: business. And then the church.

speaker: SS
words: Did your father identify much with Moscow businessmen?

speaker: CS
words: No. He didn't do that. He didn't belong to the Chamber of Commerce

speaker: CS
words: when they started in Moscow. He just didn't go for that sort of thing.

speaker: CS
words: And Rotary and that sort of thing. He just didn't go for that. And

speaker: CS
words: when I was a kid I used to think Dad ought to enter into those things

speaker: CS
words: more. I don't know whether it was pressure of time or what, but he

speaker: CS
words: just didn't enter into those things. I think, too, maybe he felt that

speaker: CS
words: they kind of set a standard, and he wasn't going to conform to a stan

speaker: CS
words: dard. He had his business. I really don't know his feeling, but I

speaker: CS
words: think he felt he had to kind of conform to something that he didn't

speaker: CS
words: want to conform to.

speaker: SS
words: A way of behaving or a certain way of dealing with customers and that

speaker: SS
words: kind of thing?

speaker: CS
words: Well, no, entering into the Main Street sort of affair, you know that—

speaker: SS
